Filter: /-annotate
Example: magick img2.png -gravity center -pointsize 36 -fill black -annotate +50+100 "Hello, World!" output.png
Result: Places the text "Hello, World!" at coordinates (50, 100) with black fill, centered on the image.

Example: magick img2.png -gravity south -font Arial -pointsize 48 -fill blue -stroke white -annotate 45 "Rotated Text" output_rotated.png
Result: Adds blue text with a white stroke, rotated 45 degrees, placed near the bottom.

Example: magick img2.png -gravity northwest -pointsize 28 -fill yellow -undercolor '#00000080' -annotate +10+10 "Annotated Text" output_with_undercolor.png
Result: Adds yellow text with a semi-transparent black undercolor in the top-left corner of the image.

Example: magick img2.png -font Times-New-Roman -pointsize 40 -fill red -background none -annotate +30+30 "Custom Font Text" output_custom_font.png
Result: Uses a custom font (Times-New-Roman) with red text and no background.
Places multiline text at the center of the image.
Adds the text "Sample Text" without rotation.
Annotates the image with text rotated 45 degrees.
Uses a specific font size for annotation.
Places text at specific coordinates (50, 100).
Adds text with a semi-transparent red background (undercolor).
Places the text at the center of the image using gravity.
Annotates with an outline around the text.
Annotates with multiple lines of text.

Example: magick img2.png -font Arial -annotate 0 "Custom Font Text" output_font.png
Result: Annotates the image using the Arial font.
Annotates the image with blue-colored text.

Filter command: Annotation with Custom Font and Size:
Example: magick img2.png -font Times-New-Roman -pointsize 30 -annotate 0 "Times New Roman Text" output_custom_font.png
Result: Uses the "Times-New-Roman" font and a custom size of 30 for the text.
Annotates text with a semi-transparent red color.

Example: magick img2.png -fill white -annotate 0 "Text with Shadow" -shadow 80x3+5+5 output_shadow.png
Result: Adds a shadow to the annotated text.
Annotates with a 10-pixel spacing between letters.
Shears the text at a 45-degree angle.

Example: magick img2.png -draw "text 100,100 'Text Along Path'" -annotate 0 "Path Text" output_path.png
Result: Annotates text along a specified path (in this case, using basic drawing).

Filter: /-black-threshold
Result: Any pixel value less than 25% brightness will be set to black, and values above will be unchanged.
Pixels darker than the specified hex value (#808080, which is medium gray) are set to black.
Pixels below the RGB value (128,128,128) will turn black, and other pixels will remain as is.

Filter command: Black Threshold with Specific Gray Value:
Result: Any pixel below 50% gray (mid-gray) is set to black, while brighter pixels stay the same.
All pixels darker than gray (default gray) will become black.

Filter:
/-blur
 radius*sigma lower value indicates higher blur (radius)
 higher indicates higher sigma(sigma)
Filter command: Simple Blur with Radius and Sigma:
Result: This applies a Gaussian blur with a sigma of 5, resulting in a moderate blur across the entire image. The radius is automatically computed based on the sigma.

Filter command: Blur with Explicit Radius and Sigma:
Result: A Gaussian blur is applied with a radius of 3 and a sigma of 10. The image becomes heavily blurred, with the higher sigma creating a more pronounced blur effect.
A very light blur is applied using a sigma of 1. The image will appear slightly softened, with details still largely preserved.
A very strong Gaussian blur is applied with a sigma of 20, causing the image to become heavily blurred, making fine details almost unrecognizable.
A moderate blur is applied with a radius of 2 and a sigma of 5. The result will be a blur effect that is more localized due to the smaller radius but still noticeable.
A blur is applied with just the radius set to 4 (without explicitly setting sigma). This results in ImageMagick computing a suitable sigma automatically, giving a balanced blur effect.

Filter:
/-border
 a*b a represents left and right side b represent top and bottom
Result: Adds a 10-pixel wide border on all sides of the image using the default color (usually black). The image will have equal borders around it.
Adds a 20-pixel wide border to the left and right, and a 5-pixel wide border to the top and bottom. The result is a rectangular border with different dimensions.
A thin 1-pixel border is applied around the image. This adds a very subtle outline around the image, barely noticeable but giving a fine edge.
A thick 50-pixel wide border is added uniformly around the image, creating a bold frame-like appearance.
Adds a border that is 5 pixels wide on the left and right and 30 pixels tall on the top and bottom. This results in a vertically prominent border that elongates the image.
Adds a 10-pixel border only to the top and bottom, with no border on the left and right sides. This creates a border effect that emphasizes the vertical edges of the image.
Adds a 5-pixel border to the top and bottom only, with no border on the left and right. This adds slight vertical padding to the image.

Filter command: Border with No Height (Horizontal Border Only):
Result: Adds a 15-pixel wide border on the left and right sides only, with no border at the top and bottom. This highlights the horizontal edges of the image.

Filter: -charcoal
Result: Applies a charcoal drawing effect with a radius and sigma of 1, creating a light sketch-like appearance with soft edges.
Applies a moderate charcoal effect with a radius and sigma of 5. This results in a stronger sketch effect with more pronounced lines and texture.
A strong charcoal effect is applied, turning the image into a highly textured sketch with heavy strokes, resembling a bold charcoal drawing.
A very subtle charcoal effect, with fine lines and minimal texture. The image will retain much of its original detail but appear lightly sketched.
Applies a barely noticeable charcoal effect with a radius of 0.2. The image will appear very slightly sketched, almost imperceptibly altering the original.

Filter:
/-chop
 x * y a represents left and right side b represent top and bottom
Result: Removes 100 pixels from the width of the image, effectively chopping off 100 pixels from the left or right side (depending on gravity settings), leaving the height unchanged.
Removes 100 pixels from the height of the image, chopping off the top or bottom portion of the image, while leaving the width unchanged.
Removes 50 pixels from the width and 150 pixels from the height of the image, cutting off both horizontally and vertically.
Removes 50 pixels from both the width and height of the image, effectively trimming equal parts from all sides and creating a smaller, centered version of the image.

Filter:
/-clip
 .svg file format is also used here
Result: Applies the clipping path from the image's internal vector data. This will mask out areas outside the clipping path, leaving only the clipped region visible.
Applies a clip inside the specified path and inverts the selection, showing only the area inside the clipping path, and hiding everything outside.

Filter command: Clip with Automatic Path from Image Metadata:
Result: Uses the clipping path stored in the image metadata (such as in a TIFF file) to define which part of the image is displayed. The rest of the image outside the path is discarded.

Filter command: Clip with Embedded Clipping Path (Vector Graphics):
Result: Applies the clip based on vector data from an SVG file, showing only the area inside the vector-defined clipping path while removing the outside portions.

Filter: /-clip-path
Result: Applies the clipping path, then resets it and reapplies the clipping based on the image's metadata, ensuring the clipping path is consistently enforced.
Applies a specific clipping path identified by #path1 from an SVG file. Only the part of the image inside the path path1 will be visible, and the rest will be masked out.
Combines multiple clipping paths from the SVG file (#path1 and #path2), showing only the areas inside both paths and masking everything else.
Removes any applied clipping path, returning the image to its full size and visibility without any masked areas.

Filter: -clip-mask
Result: Applies the specified mask (mask.png) to clip the image. Only the parts of the image corresponding to the white areas in the mask will be visible, while the rest is hidden.
Clips the image using the specified mask, then removes the mask, effectively applying the mask and then resetting the image's full visibility.
Removes any applied clip mask, returning the image to its full, unclipped state.

Filter:
/-colors
 takes only one value lower value takes toward black and white while higher value increases sharpness in image
Result: Reduces the number of colors in the image to 16, creating a simplified, posterized version with fewer shades. This can create a more abstract or "flat" look.
Reduces the color palette of the image to 256 colors, which is commonly used in GIF images. The image will still retain most of its detail but with a limited color range.
Converts the image into a black-and-white version by reducing the color palette to 2 colors. This gives the image a stark, high-contrast look.
Reduces the number of colors in the image to 64, producing a posterized effect that simplifies the image without making it entirely abstract.
Reduces the image to 8 distinct colors, creating a highly stylized, cartoonish effect. This is often used for creative or retro looks.
Reduces the image to 128 colors, which will still retain much of the original image detail but with a noticeable reduction in color gradients and smoothness.
Reduces the number of colors in the image to 32, resulting in a posterized appearance with obvious bands of color and reduced detail.
Reduces the color palette to just 4 colors, creating an extreme posterization effect. The image will have a very abstract, almost comic-book style.
Converts the image into a monochrome version, resulting in an entirely black image or white image depending on the dominant color in the input image.
Reduces the image to 512 colors. This preserves much of the image’s detail and gradient but will slightly reduce color depth and smoothness.

Filter:
/-colorize
 RGB- red blue green
 minimum 0
 maximum 0
Result: Applies a full red overlay to the image, turning it into shades of red while preserving underlying details.
Adds a blue overlay across the entire image, giving it a blue-toned effect.
Tints the image with a 50% green overlay, blending green with the original colors without overpowering them.
Creates a yellow-toned image by combining equal parts of red and green overlay at 50% intensity.

Filter:
/-colorspace 
 -colorspace value
Result: Converts the image to the CMY (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow) colorspace, often used in printing where these three colors are combined to create various hues.
Output: Changes the image to HCL (Hue, Chroma, Luminance) colorspace, which is perceptually uniform and useful for certain color adjustments.
Output: Applies a perceptually uniform HCLp colorspace, similar to HCL but better suited for smooth color transitions.
Output: Changes the image to HSI (Hue, Saturation, Intensity), often used for easier color manipulation based on intensity.
Output: Converts the image to HSV colorspace (Hue, Saturation, Value), often used in digital graphics for color adjustments.
Output: Changes the image to Jzazbz colorspace, a perceptually uniform model used for high-dynamic-range image editing.
Output: Converts the image to LCHab, which separates color into lightness, chroma, and hue using the LAB model’s components.
Output: Converts the image to LCHuv colorspace, similar to LCHab but based on the Luv model, for consistent color adjustments.
Output: Changes the image to LMS colorspace, based on human eye sensitivity to wavelengths.
Output: Converts the image to Log colorspace, useful in image processing for a logarithmic representation of colors.
Output: Converts the image to OHTA colorspace, which is a variation of LAB, designed for color consistency in video applications.
Output: Changes the image to OkLab, a perceptually uniform colorspace gaining popularity for its accuracy in color grading.
Output: Converts the image to OkLCH, a hue, lightness, and chroma model based on OkLab for smooth color grading.
Output: Converts the image to Rec601YCbCr colorspace, used for standard-definition video with separated brightness and color data.
Output: Converts the image to Rec709YCbCr, used in high-definition video formats with improved color fidelity.
Output: Converts the image to scRGB, which supports a wider gamut than sRGB and allows negative values for HDR images.
Output: Converts the image to Transparent colorspace, often used for images where transparency handling is critical.
Output: Changes the image to xyY colorspace, which isolates luminance information from chromaticity.
Output: Converts the image to YCC colorspace, used in video compression with separated color channels.
Output: Converts the image to YDbDr, often used in the PAL broadcast standard.
Output: Converts the image to YIQ colorspace, used in NTSC color television for color separation.
Output: Converts the image to YPbPr colorspace, commonly used in analog television signals.
Output: Leaves the colorspace undefined, potentially used when the colorspace should inherit the input image’s colorspace by default.
Output: Converts the image to the HSL colorspace (Hue, Saturation, Lightness), useful for manipulating color and lightness independently.
Output: Converts the image to HSB (Hue, Saturation, Brightness), helpful in graphics applications for brightness-based color adjustments.
Output: Converts the image to RGB, the most common colorspace for digital screens, using red, green, and blue channels.
Output: Converts the image to sRGB colorspace, standard for web and digital images, ensuring consistent colors across devices.
Output: Changes the image to Lab colorspace (Lightness and color-opponent dimensions), useful for adjusting color independently of brightness.

Filter command: Convert to LMS Colorspace (Long, Medium, Short wavelengths):
Result: Output: Converts the image to LMS colorspace, based on the human eye's sensitivity to light wavelengths.
Output: Converts the image to YUV, widely used in video processing, where Y represents brightness and U/V represent color.
Output: Converts the image to YCbCr, used in video compression where Y is luminance, and Cb and Cr represent color components.

Filter:
/-color-threshold
 RGB- red green blue
 0-255
Result: Output: Only colors within the specified red range are retained; all others are turned to white.
Output: Retains colors within the specified green range, changing other pixels to white.
Output: Keeps colors within the specified blue range, converting other colors to white.
Output: Retains mid-gray tones, converting other colors to black.

Filter:
/-compose
 The -compose option sets the composition method used when combining two images, with each method blending or overlaying images in a unique way.
 Take the first image 'destination' and overlay the second 'source' image according to the current -compose setting.
Result: Output: Multiplies the colors of the overlay and base image, darkening the resulting image.
Output: Combines images by lightening the base image with the overlay, resulting in a brighter effect.
Output: Darkens or lightens the image depending on overlay colors, enhancing midtones and contrast.
Output: Keeps only the darker pixel from each image, making the resulting image darker.
Output: Keeps only the lighter pixel from each image, resulting in a lighter overall image.
Output: Shows the difference between each pixel, producing a high-contrast, unique effect.
Output: Adds pixel values together, increasing brightness and intensity.
Output: Subtracts pixel values, which darkens the image depending on the overlay.
Output: Divides the base image by the overlay, creating unique brightness and contrast effects.
Output: Creates an inverse blend for a more subtle version of the Difference composition.
Output: Uses multiply or screen depending on overlay brightness, adding intense contrast.
Output: Darkens or lightens the base depending on the overlay, resulting in a softer, more natural look.
Output: Brightens the base image based on overlay colors, increasing highlights.
Output: Darkens the base image based on overlay colors, enhancing shadows.
Output: Adds images in a linear way, useful for high-brightness effects.
Output: Darkens by subtracting base from overlay, producing a deep, intense blend.
Output: Produces intense contrast, either darkening or brightening depending on the overlay.
Output: Replaces colors in the base image with those from the overlay in a pin-pointed way, creating bold edges.
Output: Adjusts brightness based on overlay, creating a high-contrast, linear effect.
Output: Drastically blends colors, reducing them to black, white, and primary colors.
Output: Combines images by transferring the luminance from the overlay to the base image.
Output: Adds pixel values, increasing brightness with a glowing effect.
Output: Subtracts overlay pixels from the base, creating a darker result.
Output: Combines the images with a binary XOR operation, creating a unique blend with each differing pixel.
Output: Places overlay image atop the base image where pixels overlap.
Output: Places the overlay image over the base image, making the overlay the primary image.
Output: Uses the overlay to mask parts of the base image, creating transparency.
Output: Keeps only parts of the base image not covered by the overlay, effectively cutting out the overlap.
Output: Removes the pixels from the overlap area, creating a clear or transparent result.
Output: Uses overlay to mask the destination, retaining only the intersection.
Output: Transfers the overlay image's opacity onto the base image for blending transparency.

Filter:
/-contrast
 Increase the contrast in an image by stretching the range of intensity values.
 - indicates increase
 + indicates decrease
Result: Output: Increases the contrast of the image by one level, making colors more vivid.
Output: Decreases the contrast of the image by one level, making colors more muted.
Output: Applies multiple levels of increased contrast, significantly boosting vividness and depth in colors.
Output: Applies multiple levels of decreased contrast, creating a very softened and muted effect.
Output: Increases and then decreases contrast, resulting in a slight change as each effect partly offsets the other.
Output: Applies alternating contrast changes, enhancing the contrast and then softening it for a balanced effect with some enhanced details.
Output: Applies a maximum of five contrast-increasing levels, making colors intensely vivid and details sharper.
1. Output: Applies a maximum of five contrast-decreasing levels, creating a very soft and pastel-like effect by muting colors and reducing sharpness.

Filter:
/-convolve
 apply a variety of effects, from blurring and sharpening to edge detection and creative distortions, allowing versatile transformations based on kernel matrices
 should be in form of 3 5 7 square
Result: Output: Applies a 3x3 kernel of 1s, creating a mild blur across the image.
Output: Detects edges in the image by enhancing boundaries and creating a high-contrast outline effect.
Output: Sharpens the image by enhancing edges, adding more definition to details.
Output: Applies a Gaussian blur, creating a softer, out-of-focus look.
Output: Adds an embossed effect, creating a three-dimensional relief impression on the image.
Output: Detects vertical edges, emphasizing vertical lines and boundaries in the image.
Output: Detects horizontal edges, emphasizing horizontal lines and boundaries.
Output: Applies a Laplacian filter to detect edges, resulting in a high-contrast edge-detected image.
Output: Enhances edges with less intensity, adding subtle contrast to lines and details.
Output: Detects edges along diagonal directions, emphasizing diagonal lines within the image.
Output: Applies a Sobel filter for edge detection, enhancing both horizontal and vertical edges in the image.
Output: Applies an averaging or box blur across a 3x3 area, softening the image uniformly.
Output: Creates a motion blur effect along the horizontal axis, simulating movement across the image.
Output: Creates a vertical motion blur effect, simulating movement vertically in the image.
Output: Enhances contrast in the image, highlighting transitions between light and dark areas.

Filter:
/-crop
 allows you to extract a rectangular region from an image, defined by its width, height, and an optional offset.
Result: Crops a 100x100 pixel square from the center of the image.
Crops a 150x150 pixel area starting at coordinates (50, 50).
Crops the image to the center 50% of its width and height.
Crops a 200x200 pixel area centered in the image.
Crops a 250x250 pixel area from the top-left corner of the image.
Crops a 300x300 pixel area from the bottom-right corner of the image.
Crops the image maintaining a 4:3 aspect ratio based on original dimensions.
Crops a 300x200 pixel area starting from coordinates (50, 50).

Filter command: Crop to Aspect Ratio with Offset
Result: Crops the image to a 16:9 aspect ratio starting from (10, 10).
Crops a 100x100 pixel area and removes any virtual canvas offset.
Crops a 200x200 pixel area and then resizes it to 100x100 pixels.

Filter command: Crop to a Square from the Center
Result: Crops a square (150x150 pixels) from the center of the image.
Crops a 250x250 pixel area and saves the output as a PNG file.

Filter command: Crop with Fixed Aspect Ratio and Size
Result: Crops to a 300x200 pixel area and resizes to maintain the same dimensions.
Crops a 200x200 pixel area from (10, 10) and saves with a transparent background.

Filter:
/-cycle 
 allows you to control how the images are cycled when using the magick command.
Result: Sets the cycle point to the first image (index 0).
Advances the cycle point by 1, cycling through the images.
Moves the cycle point back by 1, cycling through the images.

Filter command: Cycle Through Images with Step Size
Result: Sets the cycle point to the third image (index 2).
Advances the cycle point by 5, allowing for multiple cycling.
Cycles to the fifth-to-last image in the list.

Filter:
/-despeckle
 Reduce the speckles{mark with a large number of small spots or patches of colour} within an image
Result: Applies a despeckling filter to remove noise from the image.
Resizes the image to 200x200 pixels and then applies the despeckling filter.
Applies a slight blur before despeckling to further reduce noise.
Enhances the image contrast before despeckling to improve output quality.
Detects edges in the image before applying the despeckling filter.
Applies a Gaussian filter to the image followed by despeckling.

Filter:
/-draw
 The -draw option is versatile and allows for a wide range of drawing operations directly on images.
 
 The resulting output.jpg will display the effects of the drawing operations specified in each command.
Result: Draws a line from coordinates (10,10) to (100,100) on the image.
Draws a rectangle with corners at (50,50) and (150,150).
Draws a circle with center at (100,100) and radius determined by (120,120).
Draws an ellipse centered at (100,100) with radii of 50 and 30.
Draws a polygon connecting the specified vertices.
Draws the text "Hello World" at coordinates (10,30) on the image.
Overlays 'overlay.png' at coordinates (0,0) on the image.
Composites 'overlay.png' over the input image starting at (0,0).
Draws a path from (50,50) to (100,100) using SVG path commands.
Draws a filled blue rectangle from (50,50) to (150,150).
Draws a filled red circle centered at (100,100).

Example: magick input.jpg -draw "stroke black stroke-width 3 line 10,10 100,100" output.jpg
Result: Draws a black line with a stroke width of 3.
Draws an arc centered at (100,100) with a radius of 50.
Draws a Bezier curve connecting the specified control points.

Filter:
/-draw
 Each command reflects different drawing capabilities, allowing you to manipulate images creatively.
 Annotate an image with one or more graphic primitives.
Result: Draws a single point at coordinates (10,10) on the image.
Draws a line from (10,10) to (100,100) on the image.
Draws a rectangle with corners at (50,50) and (150,150).
Draws a rectangle with rounded corners at the specified dimensions.
Draws an arc from (50,50) to (150,150) covering the specified angles.
Draws an ellipse centered at (100,100) with the specified radii.
Draws a circle centered at (100,100) with a radius determined by (120,120).
Draws a series of connected lines between the specified points.
Draws a closed shape connecting the specified vertices.
Draws a Bezier curve using the specified control points.
Draws a path using SVG commands.
Overlays 'overlay.png' at (0,0) on the image.
Draws a filled blue rectangle.
Draws a filled red circle.

Example: magick input.jpg -draw "stroke black stroke-width 3 line 10,10 100,100" output.jpg
Result: Draws a black line with a stroke width of 3.
Draws the text "Hello World" at (10,30).

Filter:
/-edge radius
 The -edge option applies an edge detection filter to the input image, highlighting the boundaries of objects within the image.
 
 The resulting output.jpg will display the edges detected based on the specified radius.
Result: Detects edges in the image using a 1-pixel radius.
Detects edges in the image using a 3-pixel radius.
Detects edges in the image using a 5-pixel radius.
Detects edges in the image using a 10-pixel radius.
Detects edges and sets the background color to white.

Filter:
/-emboss
 The -emboss option creates a three-dimensional appearance on the image by simulating light and shadow effects based on pixel values.
 
 The resulting output.jpg will display the embossed version of the input image.
Result: Applies a basic emboss effect to the image with a radius of 1.
Applies an emboss effect with a radius of 2 for more pronounced results.
Applies a strong emboss effect with a radius of 3 for a dramatic look.
Applies an emboss effect with a custom radius of 5.

Filter:
/-enhance
 Apply a digital filter to enhance a noisy image.
 The -enhance option automatically adjusts the image to improve its overall appearance without requiring specific parameters.
Result: Enhances the overall quality of the image, improving brightness and contrast.
Enhances the image after applying a contrast adjustment.
Enhances the image and normalizes brightness levels for better contrast.
Enhances the image and sharpens it for increased detail.
Enhances and applies a threshold of 50%, resulting in a binary image.
Enhances and applies a threshold of 70% for more defined edges.

Filter:
/-equalize
 The -equalize option enhances the contrast of the image by adjusting the histogram, making the image's brightness levels more uniform.
 Perform histogram equalization on the image channel-by-channel.
Result: Equalizes the histogram of the image, enhancing contrast.
Equalizes the image in RGB color space for better color balance.
Converts to grayscale and then equalizes the image histogram.
Equalizes the image after applying a threshold of 50%.
Equalizes the image within a specified range of pixel values (10% to 90%).

Filter:
/-evaluate operator value
 
 Alter channel pixels by evaluating an arithmetic, relational, or logical expression.
 The -evaluate command allows you to apply various mathematical operations to each pixel in the image, providing significant flexibility for image adjustments.
Result: Adds 50 to each pixel value, increasing brightness.
Subtracts 30 from each pixel value, decreasing brightness.
Multiplies each pixel value by 1.5, enhancing brightness.
Divides each pixel value by 2, reducing brightness.
Converts pixels to black and white based on a threshold of 128.
Converts pixels below 50 to black, retaining others.
Converts pixels above 200 to white, retaining others.
Sets pixels to the maximum of 100 or the current pixel value.
Sets pixels to the minimum of 50 or the current pixel value.
Applies Gaussian noise to the image.
Applies Laplacian noise to the image.
Applies sine transformation to pixel values with a frequency of 45.
Applies cosine transformation to pixel values with a frequency of 45.
Applies logarithm to each pixel value, adjusting brightness.
Applies square root transformation to each pixel value.
Calculates the root mean square of the pixel values.
Raises each pixel value to the power of 2, enhancing brightness.
Applies a binary AND operation with the value 128 on each pixel.
Applies a binary OR operation with the value 128 on each pixel.
Applies a binary XOR operation with the value 128 on each pixel.
Shifts pixel values left by 1 bit, effectively multiplying by 2.
Shifts pixel values right by 1 bit, effectively dividing by 2.

Filter:
/-extent 
 geometry
 Set the image size and offset.
 The -extent command is used to resize the canvas of an image without changing its actual dimensions. It can be particularly useful for creating padding around an image.
 The -gravity option determines how the original image is positioned on the new canvas.
 The -background option specifies the color used for the newly created area when extending the canvas.
Result: Resizes the canvas to 800x600 pixels, keeping the image centered.
Extends canvas to 800x600 pixels, filling with white background.
Resizes canvas to 800x600 pixels, anchoring at the top (North).
Extends canvas to 800x600 pixels, filling with blue background.
Adjusts the canvas size negatively; pixels will be cropped.
Extends canvas to 800x600 pixels, centered with a gray background.

Filter:
/-flip
 Create a mirror image
 reflect the scanlines in the vertical direction. The image will be mirrored upside-down.
Result: Flips the image vertically (top to bottom).
Flips the image vertically and saves it as a PNG file.
Flips the image vertically and overwrites the original file.
Flips the image vertically and saves it as a TIFF file.
Flips the image vertically with a quality setting of 90%.
Flips the image vertically and provides verbose output about the process.

Filter:
/-flop
 Create a mirror image.
 Reflect the scanlines in the horizontal direction, just like the image in a vertical mirror.
Result: Flops the image horizontally (left to right).
Flops the image horizontally and saves it as a PNG file.
Flops the image horizontally and overwrites the original file.
Flops the image horizontally and saves it as a TIFF file.
Flops the image horizontally with a quality setting of 90%.

Filter:
/-floodfill {+-}x{+-}y color
 Floodfill the image with color at the specified offset.
 Flood fill starts from the given 'seed point' which is not gravity affected.
 used to fill a contiguous area of pixels starting from a specified coordinate with a specific color.
Result: Fills the area starting from pixel (0,0) with the color red.
Fills from pixel (10,10) with blue, preserving transparency.
Fills the area at (50,50) with green, using a tolerance of 10.
Fills the area at (20,20) with the specified hex color (#FF5733).

Filter command: Flood Fill with Specific Fill Area
Result: Fills the area at (30,30) with yellow and applies paint effect.
Fills the area at (40,40) with cyan, limiting fill to 50% of the region.

Filter:
/-frame geometry
 Surround the image with a border or beveled frame.
 
 used to add a decorative frame around an image.
 You can customize the frame thickness, color, and background options to suit your needs.
Result: Adds a simple frame of 10 pixels around the image.
Adds a blue frame of 5 pixels around the image.
Adds a green inner border frame of 15 pixels around the image.
Adds a frame of 20 pixels on the width and 10 pixels on the height.
Adds a red frame with specific thickness around the image.
Adds a transparent frame of 10 pixels around the image.
Adds a complex frame with white background and black border.

Filter:
/-fx expression
 Apply a mathematical expression to an image or image channels.
 R-red G-green B-blue
 You can manipulate color channels and apply complex transformations using various functions and operations.
Result: Reduces the red channel to half its original value.
Adjusts brightness by averaging the RGB channels and scaling down.
Increases the red channel intensity by 20%.
Converts image to grayscale using luminance calculation.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 3.
Creates a negative of the image by subtracting the red channel from 1.
Creates a composite of the RGB values.
Applies a threshold, setting red channel to 0 or 1 based on a condition.
Isolates and outputs only the red channel.

Filter:
/-gamma value
 Level of gamma correction.
 The -gamma option adjusts the gamma of an image, affecting its brightness and contrast.
 A value less than 1 darkens the image, while a value greater than 1 brightens it.
Result: Adjusts the image gamma to 2.2, brightening it overall.
Range: Reasonable values extend from 0.8 to 2.3. Gamma less than 1.0 darkens the image and gamma greater than 1.0 lightens it

Result: Adjusts the image gamma to 0.5, darkening it overall.
Adjusts the image gamma to 3.0, significantly brightening it.
Applies different gamma values to R, G, and B channels respectively.
Adjusts the gamma to the default value of 1.0.
Applies multiple gamma corrections, resulting in a complex image adjustment.

Filter:
/-gaussian-blur radius
 Blur the image with a Gaussian operator.
 The -gaussian-blur option uses a specified radius and sigma to determine the intensity of the blur effect.
 The first value is the radius, which controls the size of the area around each pixel to consider when blurring, and the second value is sigma, which defines how much to blur.
 If the radius is set to 0, it will automatically calculate the appropriate radius based on the sigma.
Result: Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 0 and a sigma of 2.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 0 and a sigma of 5 for a softer effect.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 3 and a sigma of 2.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 5 and a sigma of 5.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a default radius and a sigma of 2.
Applies a Gaussian blur with a radius of 4 and default sigma.
Applies multiple Gaussian blurs for enhanced softening effects.

Filter:
/-grayscale method
 Convert image to grayscale.
 The -grayscale option provides several methods for converting a color image to grayscale, allowing for flexibility based on the desired outcome.
 The different methods use various formulas to calculate the luminance or average values from the RGB channels to create the grayscale image.
 Threshold and range are typically not applicable as the output is a single-channel grayscale image where pixel values range from 0 (black) to 255 (white).
Result: Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the Rec. 601 Luma formula (non-linear RGB).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the Rec. 601 Luminance formula (linear RGB).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the Rec. 709 Luma formula (non-linear RGB).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the Rec. 709 Luminance formula (linear RGB).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the brightness method (maximum RGB channel value).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the lightness method (average of minimum and maximum RGB values).
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the average of the RGB values.
Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the mean square formula.

Filter command: Grayscale Conversion using Root Mean Square (RMS)
Result: Output: Converts the image to grayscale using the root mean square formula.

Filter:
/-implode factor
 Implode image pixels about the center.
 The implode effect is used to simulate the effect of compressing an image's pixels toward the center.
 Positive values shrink the image, while negative values expand it.
Result: Output: Reduces the image size by 50% using implode effect.
Output: Fully implodes the image, compressing all pixels to the center.
Output: Increases the implode effect, collapsing pixels further.
Output: Applies negative implode effect, expanding the image.

Filter:
/-immutable
 Make image immutable.
 The -immutable flag is useful for protecting images from accidental edits.
 Setting an image as immutable is a simple way to ensure its integrity during processing.
Result: Output: Marks the image as immutable, preventing changes.
Output: Resets the immutable property, allowing changes.

Filter:
/-integral
 Calculate the sum of values (pixel values) in the image.
 The -integral command helps speed up processing tasks, particularly for convolution and filtering.
 It can be used for single or multiple color channels.
Result: Output: Computes the integral image, useful for quick calculations.
Output: Computes the integral for a specific channel (red).

Filter command: Compute Integral Image with Multiple Channels
Result: Output: Computes integral images for multiple channels.

Filter:
/-kmeans colors{xiterations}{+tolerance}
 Kmeans (iterative) color reduction (e.g. -kmeans 5x300+0.0001).
 The -kmeans command is used for color quantization, effectively reducing the number of distinct colors in an image.
 The number of clusters can be adjusted according to the desired output.
Result: Output: Clusters the image into 5 colors.
Output: Clusters the image into 5 colors with 10 iterations.
Output: Clusters the image into 5 colors using sRGB color space.
Output: Clusters the image into 5 colors and applies dithering.
Output: Clusters the image into 5 colors and uses the existing colors.

Filter:
/-label
 The -label command is used to annotate images with text.
 You can customize font, position, and background color for the label.
Result: Output: Adds the label "Sample Label" to the image.
Output: Adds the label "Sample" with Arial font to the image.
Output: Adds the label "Sample" at the bottom of the image.
Output: Adds the label "Sample" with a light blue background.
Output: Adds the label "Sample" with a transparent background.

Filter:
/-lat 
 applies local adaptive thresholding for high-contrast results.
 Parameters: <width>x<height>+<threshold>, where threshold is a percentage
Result: Output: Applies adaptive thresholding with a 15x15 neighborhood and 10% threshold.
Output: Increases threshold sensitivity with a 20x20 neighborhood and 20% threshold.
Output: Thresholds with a 10x10 neighborhood and 5% threshold for finer detail.
Output: Applies thresholding with a 30x30 neighborhood and 15% threshold for broad adjustments.
Output: Increases sensitivity to variations with a 25% threshold on a 25x25 neighborhood.

Filter:
/-level 
 level adjustments are made using the format -level <black point>,<white point>[,<gamma>].
 The command modifies image brightness, contrast, and gamma for a broad range of tonal adjustments.
Result: Output: Adjusts the image’s levels from full black to full white range.
Output: Expands highlights beyond normal, enhancing brightness.
Output: Compresses shadows while keeping highlights at max range.
Output: Focuses on midtones by limiting shadows and highlights, balancing contrast.
Output: Adjusts gamma to 1.2, slightly enhancing midtones without altering black or white points.
Output: Creates strong contrast by reducing both shadow and highlight ranges.

Filter:

 this option restricts colors in input.jpg to those in the provided color map file.

 Apply a median filter to the image.
 Select the 'middle' value from all the surrounding pixels.
 smoothens noise while preserving edges.
 Higher radius values create more pronounced smoothing effects.

Uses a 5-pixel radius for more intense noise reduction and smoothing.
Minimal smoothing with a 1-pixel radius for slight noise reduction.
Extreme noise reduction with a high median radius for very smooth results.

Filter:
/-modulate brightness[,saturation,hue]
 Vary the brightness, saturation, and hue of an image.
 -modulate adjusts brightness, saturation, and hue using the format -modulate brightness,saturation,hue.
 Values are in percentages (0–200% for brightness/saturation; 0–360° for hue).
Result: Adjusts brightness to 120% without changing hue or saturation.
Increases saturation to 150% without altering brightness or hue.
Adjusts hue by 180 degrees while keeping brightness and saturation unchanged.

 Transform the image to black and white.

Applies Floyd-Steinberg dithering for smoother monochrome conversion.
Converts to monochrome without any dithering effect.

Filter:
/-negate
 Replace each pixel with its complementary color.
 
 The red, green, and blue intensities of an image are negated. White becomes black, yellow becomes blue, etc. 
 command can target specific color channels or apply to the entire image.
 This operation is useful for creating negative images or adjusting color contrast.
Result: Inverts the colors of the image, turning black to white and vice versa.
Negates only the red channel of the image.
Negates the red and green channels.
Inverts colors and keeps transparency intact.
Inverts colors and writes the output image in a single step.

Filter:
/-normalize
 Increase the contrast in an image by stretching the range of intensity values.
 The intensity values are stretched to cover the entire range of possible values. While doing so, black-out at most 2% of the pixels and white-out at most 1% of the pixels.
Result: Normalizes the image by scaling pixel values to the full range of colors.
Normalizes and clips pixel values to avoid overflow.
Normalizes only the red channel separately.
Enhances contrast by applying auto-leveling after normalization.

Filter:
/-opaque color
 Change this color to the fill color within the image.
 It is useful for replacing colors in an image, making them opaque or transparent as needed.
Result: Changes all blue pixels to opaque.
Changes red pixels to opaque while making white pixels transparent.
Replaces pixels matching the specified RGB color with opaque.
Makes black pixels opaque and blue pixels transparent.

 option is particularly useful for reducing color banding and enhancing the visual quality of images with limited color palettes.
